questions.question: Explain the different states of a process with a state transition diagram.
keyPoints: Define the five process states: New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated
Explain what happens in each state
Draw and explain transitions between states: New→Ready, Ready→Running, Running→Ready, Running→Waiting, Waiting→Ready, Running→Terminated

questions.question: What is Process Control Block (PCB)? Explain its main components.
keyPoints: PCB is a data structure storing process information
Components include: Process ID, Process State, Program Counter, CPU Registers
Memory management info, I/O status information, Accounting data

questions.question: Explain the concept of context switching with an example.
keyPoints: Context switching is saving/restoring process state
It allows multiple processes to share CPU
Involves saving registers, PC, stack pointer to PCB
Example: Timer interrupt causes OS to switch from one process to another

questions.question: Differentiate between a process and a thread.
keyPoints: Process: independent execution unit with own address space
Thread: lightweight unit within process sharing memory/resources
Threads have less overhead for creation and context switching
Processes provide isolation; threads share resources

questions.question: Explain the fork() and exec() system calls in Unix process creation.
keyPoints: fork() creates new process by duplicating parent
Returns child's PID to parent, 0 to child
exec() replaces process image with new program
fork+exec is common pattern: fork creates process, exec loads new program

questions.question: What is Interprocess Communication (IPC)? Mention any two methods.
keyPoints: IPC allows processes to communicate and synchronize
Methods: Shared Memory, Message Passing, Pipes, Signals
Shared Memory: processes share memory region
Message Passing: processes exchange messages through OS

questions.question: Explain the Critical Section problem and its requirements.
keyPoints: Critical Section: code accessing shared resources
Three requirements: Mutual Exclusion (only one process in CS), Progress (processes not in CS can participate), Bounded Waiting (finite waiting time)
Solution ensures synchronized access to shared resources

questions.question: Discuss the following CPU scheduling algorithms: FCFS, SJF, and Round Robin. Compare them in terms of throughput, waiting time, and turnaround time.
keyPoints: FCFS: First Come First Served, non-preemptive, convoy effect
SJF: Shortest Job First, optimal average waiting time but requires burst time prediction
Round Robin: Time-sharing, preemptive with time quantum, fair but may have high context switching
Comparison: SJF best avg waiting, FCFS simple but convoy problem, RR good for time-sharing

questions.question: Explain the producer-consumer problem and how shared memory can be used to solve it.
keyPoints: Producer creates items, Consumer consumes them
Shared buffer needed for synchronization
Problems: Race condition on buffer access, overflow/underflow
Solution: Use synchronization primitives (semaphores, mutex)
Circular buffer implementation with in and out pointers
Mutual exclusion and condition synchronization required

questions.question: What is a deadlock? Explain the necessary conditions for deadlock occurrence and methods to handle deadlocks.
keyPoints: Deadlock: processes waiting for resources held by each other
Four conditions: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait
Prevention: Break one of the four conditions
Avoidance: Banker's algorithm detects safe states
Detection: Resource allocation graph analysis
Recovery: Process termination or resource preemption

questions.question: Explain the concept of threads. Discuss multithreading models and advantages of multithreading over single threading.
keyPoints: Thread: lightweight process within a process
Thread shares code, data, and OS resources with other threads
Models: Many-to-One, One-to-One, Many-to-Many
Advantages: Responsiveness, Resource sharing, Economy (less overhead), Scalability (multi-core)
Each thread has own: Thread ID, Stack, Registers, PC
